elements.sent-2411-001: Event-B is a well known methodology for the verified design and development of systems that can be characterised as discrete transition systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Hybrid Event-B is a conservative extension that interleaves the discrete transitions of Event-B (assumed to be temporally isolated) with episodes of continuously varying state change.
elements.sent-2411-003: While a single Hybrid Event-B machine is sufficient for applications with a single locus of control, it will not do for autonomous systems, which have several loci of control by default.
elements.sent-2411-004: Multi-machine Hybrid Event-B is designed to allow the specification of systems with several loci of control.
elements.sent-2411-005: The formalism is succinctly surveyed, pointing out the subtle semantic issues involved.
elements.sent-2411-006: The multi-machine formalism is then used to specify a relatively simple incident response system, involving a controller, two drones and three responders, working in a partly coordinated and partly independent fashion to manage a putative hazardous scenario.
elements.sent-2411-007: These days, there is an inexorable drive to automate as many of the tasks that humans engage in to progress their everyday lives as possible.
elements.sent-2411-008: The motives range from gaining efficiency, to enabling genuinely new activities which would otherwise be impossible.
elements.sent-2411-009: Autonomous systems can be deployed to achieve both aims.
elements.sent-2411-010: Properly designed, not only can they enable efficiencies in activities traditionally undertaken by humans (e.g.~logistics), but they can also enable activities (e.g.~reactor core investigation) that would be lethal for humans.
elements.sent-2411-011: Proper design of autonomous systems implies the ability to place great trust in their operation, given that they act in a manner much less supervised than is the case for conventional systems.
elements.sent-2411-012: If a system is to be given responsibility for deciding its own course of action (to whatever degree is considered reasonable), it must be understood how the gamut of its decision making capabilities keep it aligned with the widest considerations that its behaviour may affect.
elements.sent-2411-013: In other words, the boundary between the autonomous system and the wider environment needs to be reliably appreciated, and the greater the creativity that the system is permitted to display in meeting its local challenges, the greater the burden of understanding that is imposed on system design to ensure that letting the system do its thing will not jeopardise either itself, or elements of the wider environment.
elements.sent-2411-014: To try to ensure this, all available techniques for enhancing system assurance may be brought to bear.
elements.sent-2411-015: As well as the traditional approaches of careful design and testing, more formal approaches, that aim to strengthen the guarantees that can be given regarding system behaviour, may be used.
elements.sent-2411-016: This highlights safety properties, the focus of the B-Method.
elements.sent-2411-017: The significant self-agency of autonomous systems means that they are often cyber-physical systems \cite{Car.Rob...:06,Tab:09,GeiBro:15 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The consequent interaction between discrete and continuous dynamics raises a challenge for verification \cite{Garo:19,Sanf:21 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The contribution of this paper is to show that the architecture and capabilities of multi-machine Hybrid Event-B are suitable for formalising the challenge just described.
elements.sent-2411-020: Hybrid Event-B was envisaged as a conservative extension of conventional Event-B that was able to accommodate continuous and smooth behaviour in a rigorous way.
elements.sent-2411-021: The foundations of the formalism were investigated in three papers \cite{BaHyEvB-I:15a,BaHyEvB-TWO:15b,BaHyEvB-3:15c , referred to below as PaperI, PaperII, PaperIII.}
elements.sent-2411-022: These may be consulted for full technical details; a less detailed overview appears in \cite{BaHyEvB-OV:24 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Of particular relevance to the present work is \cite{BaHyEvB-TWO:15b , in which the multi-machine version of the formalism was explored, arriving at a framework that copes equally comfortably with multi-machine continuous behaviour as it does with the more familiar discrete behviours more typically used in verification.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Given that autonomous systems perforce exhibit a degree of disconnect between themselves and any environment, a modelling and verification framework that captures this structural aspect in separate constructs is intrinsically useful.
elements.sent-2411-025: The main thrust of the present paper is the assertion that these aspects of the multi-machine version of Hybrid Event-B are particularly expressive and helpful in organising the exploration and verification of the complex scenarios that arise when autonomous systems are investigated.
elements.sent-2411-026: The rest of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-027: Section \ref{sec-eb+heb introduces Hybrid Event-B as a natural extension of Event-B.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{sec-heb-mult presents the main features of multiple cooperating Hybrid Event-B machines.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Section \ref{sec-inc-resp applies the multi-machine formalism to present a simple incident response system, involving a controller, two drones and three responders, working in a partly coordinated and partly independent fashion to manage a putative hazardous scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Each of the agents mentioned is specified in its own machine, and the architecture of the multi-machine formalism conveniently captured the cooperation mechanisms needed.
elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sec-verif briefly considers verification for Hybrid Event-B and of the case study, and Section \ref{sec-conc concludes.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \fbox{0.19\linewidth{ \hspace*{2ex}MACHINE~~$\bf Nodes$\rule{0pt}{2.5ex} \hspace*{2ex}SEES~~$N\!Ctx$ \hspace*{2ex}VARIABLES~~$nod$ \hspace*{2ex}INVARIANTS \hspace*{4ex}$nod \in \mathbb{P}(N\!Set)$ \raisebox{-1ex}{\rule{0pt}{2.5ex}} }} \fbox{0.22\linewidth{ \hspace*{2ex}EVENTS\rule{0pt}{2.5ex} \hspace*{4ex}$\mathit{INITIALISATION}$ \hspace*{6ex}STATUS~~ordinary \hspace*{6ex}BEGIN \hspace*{8ex}$nod := \emptyset$ \hspace*{6ex}END \raisebox{-1ex}{\rule{0pt}{2.5ex}} }} \fbox{0.28\linewidth{ \hspace*{4ex}$AddNode$\rule{0pt}{2.5ex} \hspace*{6ex}STATUS~~ordinary \hspace*{6ex}ANY~~$n$ \hspace*{6ex}WHERE~~$n \in N\!Set - nod$ \hspace*{6ex}THEN~~$nod := nod \cup \{n\}$ \hspace*{6ex}END \hspace*{2ex}END \raisebox{-1ex}{\rule{0pt}{2.5ex}} }} \fbox{0.23\linewidth{ \hspace*{2ex}CONTEXT~~$\bf N\!Ctx$\rule{0pt}{2.5ex} \hspace*{2ex}SETS~~$N\!Set$ \hspace*{2ex}CONSTANTS \hspace*{4ex}$aa , bb , cc , dd$ \hspace*{2ex}AXIOMS \hspace*{4ex}$N\!Set = \{aa , bb , cc , dd\}$ \hspace*{2ex}END \raisebox{-1ex}{\rule{0pt}{2.5ex}} }} \caption{A simple Event-B machine, together with its context.} \label{fig-eb-mch}
elements.sent-2411-033: The B-Method \cite{Bbook:1996 and Event-B in particular \cite{Ebook:2010 have, by now, a well established pedigree.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: By way of introduction, Fig.~\ref{fig-eb-mch shows a simple Event-B machine $\bf Nodes$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: A static node set $N\!Set$ is defined in the context $\bf N\!Ctx$, and a dynamic set $nod$ can add elements of $N\!Set$ to itself via the guarded event $AddNode$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The dynamics of the machine consists of the state transitions that ensue as events are executed one by one.
elements.sent-2411-037: Each possible sequence of transitions can be collected into a system trace.
elements.sent-2411-038: It is assumed that, in the real world, some non-empty interval of real world time elapses between each event occurrence.
elements.sent-2411-039: The restriction to discrete events makes the original Event-B poorly suited for investigating the hybrid and cyber-physical systems that are prevalent today.
elements.sent-2411-040: But the real-time gap assumed to be present between discrete event occurrence, makes a convenient opening for interleaving pieces of continuous behaviour into the time intervals between them, and this was how Hybrid Event-B was designed.
elements.sent-2411-041: The intuition just described results in there being two kinds of event in Hybrid Event-B.
elements.sent-2411-042: There are {\bf mode events, which specify discrete changes of state ---just as in Event-B--- and there are {\bf pliant events, which specify episodes of continuous state update.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Since we have continuous state update, a number of technical details have to be handled.
elements.sent-2411-044: Time becomes a crucial entity --- all variables now become, semantically, functions of real time (and not, as in Event-B, functions of an index into a sequence of state values).
elements.sent-2411-045: We have to have a policy about how wild, or well behaved, the functions of time are permitted to be, and for that, we demand that these functions have well defined left and right limits everywhere, and are continuous from the right.
elements.sent-2411-046: We also want to use differential equations (ODEs) to specify behaviour, so we need to restrict to functions which are piecewise solutions to ODE systems.
elements.sent-2411-047: Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-mch shows a simple Hybrid Event-B machine.}
elements.sent-2411-048: After the machine name is the TIME declaration, which names the variable used to denote real time (if needed).
elements.sent-2411-049: This permits read-only access to time in the rest of the machine.
elements.sent-2411-050: Time is synchronised (via a WHEN clause) with the start of a run in the $\mathit{INITIALISATION}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Next comes a CLOCK variable $clk$; these can be reset to some value (but are otherwise read-only) and allow time to be measured from convenient starting points.
elements.sent-2411-052: Then come the PLIANT and VARIABLES declarations.
elements.sent-2411-053: The former introduces the pliant variables, while the latter introduces the mode variables.
elements.sent-2411-055: These are required to hold {\it at all times , so, for example the invariant `$x \in \mathbb{R}$' means that the function of time that is the semantic value of the variable $x$ is a {\it real valued function of time.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: A little thought shows that this is the same convention used in Event-B (aside from time being real rather than an index).
elements.sent-2411-057: Accepting that $x \in \mathbb{R}$ holds at all times, does not help in knowing how values of $x$ at `nearby' times are related.
elements.sent-2411-058: To keep things under control, we must insist that the semantic value of $x$ is, again, at least piecewise, a solution to some ODE system.
elements.sent-2411-060: The mode events (STATUS~ordinary) are {\it exactly as in Event-B, and the syntax of $\mathit{MoEv}$ in Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-mch shows which variables can be updated by a mode event, using the generic $\mathit{BApred}$ syntax.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The pliant events (STATUS~pliant), such as $\mathit{PliEv}$, contain novel features and require more care.
elements.sent-2411-062: The WHERE guard is as in Event-B, imposing enabledness conditions that involve mode variables only.
elements.sent-2411-063: The INIT guard imposes enabledness conditions that involve the pliant variables (but which can also involve mode variables, if needed).
elements.sent-2411-064: The actual state update is specified in the COMPLY and SOLVE clauses.
elements.sent-2411-065: Since the purpose of a pliant event is to specify state update over an extended period of time, some tricky technical issues arise, which we mostly ignore in this overview.
elements.sent-2411-066: See PaperI for a full discussion.
elements.sent-2411-067: The SOLVE clause is the easiest to describe.
elements.sent-2411-068: It can contain one or more ODEs (with syntax $\mathscr{D x = \phi$) and one or more direct assignments (with syntax $y$~:=~$E$).}
elements.sent-2411-069: Provided the RHSs of these forms are as well behaved as stipulated above, no special problems arise.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, direct update to something which is already well behaved poses no problems, and integrating an ODE with well behaved RHS improves the already acceptable behaviour as regards smoothness.
elements.sent-2411-071: By contrast, the COMPLY clause imposes invariant-like constraints that must hold during the execution of the pliant event, and are indicated by $\mathit{BDApred}$, the before-during-after clause (the analogue of Event-B's $\mathit{BApred}$).
elements.sent-2411-072: Essentially, the same caveats regarding time dependent behaviours that we mentioned in the context of invariants must hold for the properties that comprise $\mathit{BDApred}$.
elements.sent-2411-073: As for almost all formal semantics of hybrid system notations, the semantics of Hybrid Event-B is operational.
elements.sent-2411-074: Starting with the $\mathit{INITIALISATION}$ mode event, mode and pliant event executions alternate within a run.
elements.sent-2411-075: Each mode event execution must enable at least one pliant event and disable all mode events --- after which a pliant event execution then takes over.
elements.sent-2411-076: Each pliant event execution runs until one of three things happens.
elements.sent-2411-077: At that moment the pliant event execution is preempted, and then some enabled mode event execution takes over.
elements.sent-2411-078: Note the language in the immediately preceding remarks.
elements.sent-2411-079: They indicate that there are choices to be made at mode$\rightarrow$pliant handover and pliant$\rightarrow$mode handover.
elements.sent-2411-080: These choices are nondeterministic among all the events that are enabled at the requisite moment.
elements.sent-2411-081: They must all be of the right kind (either all mode or all pliant), or else the run aborts.
elements.sent-2411-082: In more concrete terms, a run of a Hybrid Event-B machine looks like the following.
elements.sent-2411-083: Time $\mathcal{T}$ corresponds with a semi-infinite interval of the reals, e.g.~$\mathcal{T = \mathbb{R _{\geq 0}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The operational semantics partitions this into a sequence of non-empty, left-closed, right-open intervals $\mathcal{T \equiv \langle [t_0 \ldots t_1), [t_1 \ldots t_2), \ldots \rangle$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, if $t_0$ is the initial time point, the state of the machine's variables at $t_0$ is as specified in the $\mathit{INITIALISATION}$ mode event.
elements.sent-2411-086: During $[t_0 \ldots t_1)$, i.e.~during the set of times $t_0 \leq t < t_1$, the state evolves as specified in the pliant event chosen to execute immediately after $\mathit{INITIALISATION}$.
elements.sent-2411-087: This runs until it is preempted by some mode event which is enabled at time $t_1$.
elements.sent-2411-088: At that moment, the mode event executes, and the state changes discontinuously.
elements.sent-2411-089: The mode event's before-state is the left limit of the state evolution at $t_1$, and the mode event's after-state is the value at $t_1$ itself.. This pattern repeats.
elements.sent-2411-090: So $[t_1 \ldots t_2)$ is the duration of the next pliant event execution, after which a mode event is executed at $t_2$, followed by the next pliant event execution.. And so on.
elements.sent-2411-091: To ensure that all executions of a Hybrid Event-B machine take place as described, a (considerable) number of proof obligations (POs) need to be shown to hold.
elements.sent-2411-092: Some of these, particularly concerning mode events, closely resemble their Event-B counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-093: Others, particularly concerning pliant events, contain novel elements.
elements.sent-2411-094: In all cases though, some potentially delicate technicalities connected with continuous behaviour intrude into the formulation of the POs.
elements.sent-2411-095: We do not have space to discuss these here, so we refer to PaperI and PaperIII for details.
elements.sent-2411-096: As for all dialects of the B-Method, refinement is an important topic for Hybrid Event-B.
elements.sent-2411-097: Given that Hybrid Event-B pertains to a context in which real world time plays a significant role, there are a number of ways to formulate a refinement notion.
elements.sent-2411-098: Given further that in refinement there has to be an abstract machine and a concrete machine, the most urgent question concerns how the notions of `real world time' are related to one another in the two machines.
elements.sent-2411-099: We could allow the two notions to be a bit `elastic' with respect to one another, using something like a Skorokhod metric to measure how far apart the elasticity had stretched abstract and concrete time and behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-100: However, this was felt to be technically too obscure for a formalism like Hybrid Event-B, that was aimed at practical engineering purposes.
elements.sent-2411-101: So, for Hybrid Event-B, a more conservative approach was taken, and it became a matter of principle, that {\bf Hybrid Event-B preserves the notion of real world time through refinement .}
elements.sent-2411-102: In fact, it can be said that, although it is never stated, this is an assumption that also typically applies to Event-B itself, insofar as Event-B models are intended to reflect phenomena in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-103: If we add to this principle the desire that Hybrid Event-B refinement does not disturb the essential structural features of Event-B refinement, which amounts to saying that Hybrid Event-B refinement of mode events works as does refinement of events in Event-B, a quite strong set of constraints is generated.
elements.sent-2411-105: The closely spaced vertical bars represent the before- and after-states of mode event executions, while the horizontal lines between them represent the continuous state change of pliant event executions.
elements.sent-2411-106: For the moment let us forget the occurrences of $\mathit{PliEv}$\pmb{\_ and pretend that the horizontal lines just represent the passage of time, i.e.~that we are dealing with Event-B refinement in a situation in which real world time passes between event executions.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The traditional Event-B refinement proof obligations tie together what happens at abstract and concrete levels quite closely.
elements.sent-2411-108: Thus for every abstract event execution, there is a corresponding concrete event execution of its refinement (by Event-B relative deadlock freedom).
elements.sent-2411-109: Conversely, for every event execution of the concrete refinement of an abstract event, there is a corresponding abstract event execution of its abstraction (by Event-B guard strengthening).
elements.sent-2411-110: Between execution occurrences of abstract event refinements, execution occurrences of `new' events, freshly introduced at the concrete level, can appear.
elements.sent-2411-111: These must refine abstract {\sf skip s (represented by a solid vertical bar in Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-ref ).}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Transferring this verbatim to the Hybrid Event-B world says how refinement of mode events works.
elements.sent-2411-113: Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-ref thus shows abstract $\mathit{MoEvA _1$, $\mathit{MoEvA _2$, $\mathit{MoEvA _3$, which are refined to $\mathit{MoEvC _1$, $\mathit{MoEvC _2$, $\mathit{MoEvC _3$, at the same moments of time as their abstract counterparts.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Pliant events must fit round this.
elements.sent-2411-115: An immediate consequence is that the durations of abstract pliant event executions are equal to, or are partitioned by, suitable concrete pliant event execution durations --- the intervals of abstract pliant event executions are related to the intervals of concrete pliant event executions via an inverse function.
elements.sent-2411-116: Beyond this, the natural extension of the principle that abstract mode events are refined by corresponding concrete mode events implies that abstract pliant events are refined by corresponding concrete pliant events too.
elements.sent-2411-117: In Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-ref this would imply that $\mathit{PliEvA _1$ was refined by $\mathit{PliEvC _1$ and that $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ was refined by $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.1}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Without `new' concrete events, there would be little more to be said, and new mode events cause little trouble by themselves.
elements.sent-2411-119: It turns out that provided the concrete refinements of abstract events are not constrained to have the same name, there is no possible distinction between old and new pliant events.
elements.sent-2411-120: Observe that the duration of $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.1}$ in Fig.~\ref{fig-heb-ref is strictly shorter than the duration of $\mathit{PliEvA _2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: To close the gap in time at the concrete level, some pliant event, $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.2}$ must execute.}
elements.sent-2411-122: There is no alternative to the fact that $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.2}$ must refine $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ --- both events have an extended duration so there is no counterpart of the `refining of {\sf skip ' that can apply to mode events.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The main attendant issue that arises is the fact that prior to the execution of $\mathit{MoEvC _{2.1}$, $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ was executing.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: And while it was not able to change any mode variables during this period, it certainly was able to change some pliant variables, this being its main purpose.
elements.sent-2411-125: So, by the time the guards of $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.2}$ need to be checked, the abstract state is, in general, not the same as it was when the execution of $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ was launched.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: This, despite the fact that the only event that the guards of $\mathit{PliEvC _{2.2}$ might be checked against is still $\mathit{PliEvA _2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: The relevant PO resolves this by making the checking of the $iv$ guard of the corresponding abstract event optional.
elements.sent-2411-128: Checking of the abstract $grd$ guard works, since $grd$ only involves mode variables, which will not have changed during the time since $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ was launched.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Whether it makes sense or not to also check the abstract $iv$ guard is highly dependent on the details of $\mathit{PliEvA _2$ and of the rest of the abstract machine.}
elements.sent-2411-130: As for machines, a large number of proof obligations need to be discharged to ensure that a refinement behaves as just described.
elements.sent-2411-131: Again, we do not have space to discuss these here, so we refer to PaperI and PaperIII for details.
elements.sent-2411-132: In today's engineering landscape, having a design and development methodology that does not cater for separate development of components, is almost inconceivable.
elements.sent-2411-133: Therefore, since Hybrid Event-B is certainly intended for realistic formal system development, the implications of separate development, in the context of all the other constraints that the formalism imposes, must be confronted.
elements.sent-2411-134: Earlier work on combining and decomposing Event-B machines has yielded a number of schemes, based on shared variables, shared events and interfaces \cite{Abrial-EB:05,HoAb:10,HaHo:12,Butler:09,SilBu:09,Silv..:11 .}
elements.sent-2411-135: The Hybrid Event-B scheme, investigated in detail in PaperII, is based on ideas from all of these, taking into account the special needs of both mode and pliant events.
elements.sent-2411-136: When multiple syntactic constructs have to come together to make a bigger whole, a key issue is syntactic visibility: which elements of which entities are visible/readable/writable in which other entities, and what restrictions are in place to control this? This realisation was the key concept behind the introduction of the INTERFACE construct in Hybrid Event-B.
elements.sent-2411-137: An INTERFACE is a container that can hold a number of variables, the invariants that they must satisfy, and their initialisations.
elements.sent-2411-138: It is thus rather like a machine without events (or with one unstated default event simply demanding compliance with the invariants).
elements.sent-2411-139: A machine can access an interface via a CONNECTS clause or a READS clause.
elements.sent-2411-140: This allows the machine's events to inspect (READS) and update (CONNECTS) the interface's variables.
elements.sent-2411-141: The interface's invariants are aggregated with the machine's during verification.
elements.sent-2411-142: Since more than one machine can do this, we have a mechanism for sharing variables.
elements.sent-2411-143: In multi-machine Hybrid Event-B, to prevent a verification free-for-all that would impede separate working, invariants are restricted to be of two kinds.
elements.sent-2411-144: There are type I invariants (tIi's) which only mention variables from the construct (MACHINE or INTERFACE) that declares them.
elements.sent-2411-145: By themselves these are too restrictive to allow sufficiently expressive multi-machine working, so there are also type II invariants (tIIi's) which are exclusive to interfaces, and are of the form $U(u) \Rightarrow V(v)$, where $u$ are variables that belong to one interface (the tIIi's local variables and interface) and $v$ are variables that belong to a different interface (the tIIi's remote variables and interface).
elements.sent-2411-146: Type II invariants are declared in the interface containing the local variables.
elements.sent-2411-147: The remote interface of a tIIi is mentioned in the local interface using a READS declaration while the local interface of a tIIi is mentioned in the remote interface using a REFERS declaration.
elements.sent-2411-148: We give a small illustration of these principles in Fig.~\ref{fig-multi .}
elements.sent-2411-149: Small black disks represent variables, while small black squares represent tIi's.
elements.sent-2411-151: Events and invariants are connected to the variables that mention them by thin lines.
elements.sent-2411-152: Interfaces are large rectangles containing the variables and invariants they encapsulate --- there are two in Fig.~\ref{fig-multi , $\mathit{Itf1\_IF}$ and $\mathit{Itf2\_IF}$.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Machines are large rounded rectangles containing their local variables, tIi's and events --- again there are two, $\mathit{MA}$ and $\mathit{MB}$ in the figure.
elements.sent-2411-154: The very short thin line with one end free from an event in $\mathit{MB}$, is an I/O variable connected to the environment.
elements.sent-2411-155: The CONNECTS relationship between a machine and an interface is depicted by a thick dashed line.
elements.sent-2411-156: Finally, tIIi's are represented by an arrow from a variable in the local interface to a variable in the remote interface (these containing the local and remote variable subsets of the tIIi respectively).
elements.sent-2411-157: The construct that aggregates all the machines and interfaces of a development is the PROJECT construct, which is not indicated in Fig.~\ref{fig-multi .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth, keepaspectratio=true,viewport=20 0 560 250, clip=true] {Partition} \caption{Multiple machines and their interfaces.} \label{fig-multi}
elements.sent-2411-158: Refinement adds some potential complexity to the above.
elements.sent-2411-159: Additional constraints are needed to continue to prevent a verification free-for-all that would impede separate working.
elements.sent-2411-160: It is taken as unquestionable that machines and interfaces should both be capable of being refined.
elements.sent-2411-161: The restriction imposed on the refinement process is that the joint invariant that couples concrete variables to their abstract counterparts in a construct (be it a machine or an interface), should involve only the variables declared in the two constructs, and cannot involve variables declared in any other interface that either construct has access to.
elements.sent-2411-162: It is clear that such a restriction ensures that invariants proved at a high level continue to hold, suitably translated into concrete variables through the joint invariant, at lower levels of abstraction.
elements.sent-2411-163: In a single Hybrid Event-B machine, mode events and pliant events alternate.
elements.sent-2411-164: Within a single machine context, once the nuances of continuous/discrete behaviour are appreciated, it is fairly easy to work out how POs should be designed that can adequately police the alternation.
elements.sent-2411-165: Things are more complicated in a multi-machine world.
elements.sent-2411-166: We design separate machines on the basis that their activities are expected to be largely (though obviously not totally) independent.
elements.sent-2411-167: What does the alternation between mode events and pliant events amount to in such an environment? The answer requires a design decision about the multi-machine world.
elements.sent-2411-168: One guiding principle is that every machine, at all times, is executing some event (whether mode or pliant).
elements.sent-2411-169: Another is that in the purely discrete Event-B world, synchronised execution of events in multiple machines is a useful feature in some of the formulations cited above.
elements.sent-2411-170: Accordingly, the decision was taken to permit the synchronised execution of mode events via the introduction of a SYNCHronisation construct that declares that families of mode events across different machines of the same project are to be scheduled together.
elements.sent-2411-171: Of course the conjunction of the relevant guards has to permit this to be the case.
elements.sent-2411-172: Synchronised execution of events across multiple machines is of most interest in the context of {\it decomposition , in which refinement of a single abstract event has made it too unwieldy for it to be considered as a single indivisible entity any more, and the execution of different parts of it in different machines makes more sense.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Decomposition feels like the converse of composition (which is what the multi-machine mechanisms are addressing) but it is not --- the event scheduling strategies of (Hybrid) Event-B get in the way.
elements.sent-2411-174: Thus suppose abstract machine $M$ has two mode events $\mathit{EvA}$ and $\mathit{EvB}$, both simultaneously enabled.
elements.sent-2411-175: Then, the scheduling strategy of $M$ prevents the simultaneous execution of $\mathit{EvA}$ and $\mathit{EvB}$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Now suppose $\mathit{EvA}$ is decomposed into $\mathit{EvAX}$ and $\mathit{EvAY}$, and $\mathit{EvB}$ is decomposed into $\mathit{EvBX}$ and $\mathit{EvBY}$, and the $X$ parts execute in decomposed machine $\mathit{MX}$, and the $Y$ parts execute in decomposed machine $\mathit{MY}$.
elements.sent-2411-177: It is not inconceivable that in a world where the two machines schedule their events independently, $\mathit{EvAX}$ could be scheduled simultaneously with $\mathit{EvBY}$, giving the wrong semantics.
elements.sent-2411-178: It is for that reason that the SYNCH mechanism was introduced in Hybrid Event-B (following analogous mechanisms in Event-B).
elements.sent-2411-179: Decomposition of events requires further thought regarding inputs, outputs and local parameters.
elements.sent-2411-180: The design in PaperII requires that any input or output of a decomposed event is confined to one of the components (rather than being itself a synchronised activity).
elements.sent-2411-181: Local parameters are likewise assigned by one of the components and then shared with the rest, in a conceptually atomic single-writer multiple-readers rendezvous.
elements.sent-2411-182: Note that we have said nothing of synchronising or decomposing pliant events.
elements.sent-2411-183: Pliant events raise their own issues in a multi-machine world.
elements.sent-2411-184: We recall that pliant events executing in a machine typically get preempted.
elements.sent-2411-185: If there are multiple machines, the preemptions in different machines should be independent, otherwise the different machines are behaving like a single machine.
elements.sent-2411-186: This presents little problem in the real world context, but creates technical complexity for a formal operational semantics which is concerned with constructing a consistent global system trace.
elements.sent-2411-187: At a preemption in machine $\mathit{MA}$, the non-preempted other machines must be `paused' and their execution resumed after the scheduling choices in machine $\mathit{MA}$ have been resolved.
elements.sent-2411-188: And because this is a strange thing to contemplate for the physical world, the physical world consequences of any such technicalities must be invisible.
elements.sent-2411-189: This affects potential decomposition strategies for pliant events.
elements.sent-2411-190: In the light of these considerations, the strategy adopted for decomposition of pliant events is that it has to be programmed explicitly by the user.
elements.sent-2411-191: If pliant events are required to execute simultaneously in two machines, their guards must be arranged to be the same (and the same as the guards of the parent event if they constitute a decomposition), and to prevent `the wrong parts' of different decompositions from being scheduled simultaneously, no parent events in the same machine that are to be decomposed can ever be enabled simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-192: Fortunately, it is easy enough to impose static restrictions on event guards that can ensure the conditions required.
elements.sent-2411-193: A full discussion can be found in PaperII.
elements.section-2411-005: A Small Incident Response Case Study
elements.sent-2411-194: We illustrate the utility of the multi-machine Hybrid Event-B approach with a small case study.
elements.sent-2411-195: For lack of space we do not cover all aspects in equal depth, but focus on those structural elements which particularly contribute to convenient exploration of distributed and autonomous systems.
elements.sent-2411-196: The case study concerns an incident response system.
elements.sent-2411-197: This consists of a number of largely independent agents: a controller, three responders that enter a potentially hazardous area to effect some appropriate measures, and two drones, that act as communication intermediaries between the controller (which is assumed to be ground based) and the responders (which are assumed to be unable to communicate with the controller directly).
elements.sent-2411-198: Since the arena of the incident needing the response is assumed to be hazardous, two drones are provided, and they are expected to keep apart from each other, in case one of them suffers some disabling mishap.
elements.sent-2411-199: Described in such abstract terms, the case study is applicable to situations from natural and industrial disasters, through terrorist attacks, to battlefield warfighting.
elements.sent-2411-200: Each of these agents is modelled using an individual Hybrid Event-B machine, and there are additionally CONTEXTs and INTERFACEs that organise the means by which the various agents can cooperate.
elements.sent-2411-201: The ability to do this successfully results from the ability to partition the functionality needed into constructs that capture convincingly self-contained subsets, and the adequate flexibility of the cooperation mechanisms, that enables their shared goals to be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-202: These mechanisms have to cope with six independent but cooperating agents in the complete system, each potentially involving independently determined smooth state change.
elements.sent-2411-203: The essential lesson of the case study is that the design of multi-machine Hybrid Event-B permits the system to be described in such a way that experimentation with different scenarios can be easily achieved by changing just one machine and one context.
elements.sent-2411-205: In the body of this paper we focus on the machines that capture the behaviours of the agents, relegating the contexts and interfaces that contain the static declarations that support these machines to the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-206: Here and there we take some liberties with Event-B syntax for the sake of readability.
elements.sent-2411-208: These are restricted to be the introduction and removal of hazardous areas within the arena, and the $schedule$ variable is a succession (i.e.~sequence) of times at which the interventions take place.
elements.sent-2411-209: Each hazardous area has either a $SQ$uare footprint or a $CYL$indrical shape.
elements.sent-2411-210: So each is specified using a $SQ$ or $CYL$ tag $tg$, $x$ and $y$ coordinates, a size $sz$, and a height $ht$.
elements.sent-2411-211: The $\bf EnvironmentScenario\_Mch$ machine uses the $\mathit{AddHazard}$ event to introduce a fresh hazard when time reaches the first value in $schedule$, with the attributes of the hazard, written in B-Method notation as $(tg \mapsto xx \mapsto yy \mapsto sz \mapsto ht)$, being taken from the formal model's environment using the ANY clause.
elements.sent-2411-212: The $\mathit{TakeHazard}$ event removes an existing hazard from the set of current hazards $hazards$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The $schedule$ itself is statically defined in the $\bf IncidentResponse\_CTX$ context.
elements.sent-2411-214: Changing the constants in the context, and the details of the $\bf EnvironmentScenario\_Mch$ machine is all one needs to do to experiment with different incident management scenarios in this world.
elements.sent-2411-215: Below, we see the project file, $\bf IncidentResponse\_Prj$.
elements.sent-2411-216: Its purpose is to orchestrate all the components of the system.
elements.sent-2411-217: Many specific details are curtailed to save space.
elements.sent-2411-218: The first declared item is the GLOBalINVariantS file $\bf IncidentResponse\_GI$ which we discuss at the end.
elements.sent-2411-219: After that there is a list of contexts, interfaces and machines.
elements.sent-2411-220: The line `CONTEXT $\bf Drone1\_CTX$ IS' instantiates the context as a version of a generic library context for drones, $\bf Drone\_CTX$.
elements.sent-2411-221: After the WITH, there is a renaming mapping saying how identifiers in $\bf Drone\_CTX$ should be replaced to get $\bf Drone1\_CTX$.
elements.sent-2411-222: This instantiation allows the two contexts $\bf Drone1\_CTX$ and $\bf Drone2\_CTX$ to have different constants $V\!dr1$ and $V\!dr2$ for the velocities of the drones $\bf Drone1\_Mch$ and $\bf Drone2\_Mch$ that SEE the respective contexts.
elements.sent-2411-223: After the interfaces there are the machines.
elements.sent-2411-224: After these, there are the two drone instantiations $\bf Drone1\_Mch$ and $\bf Drone2\_Mch$, being instantiations of a generic $\bf Drone\_Mch$.
elements.sent-2411-225: We have suppressed the details of the instantiations, which amount to adding `1' or `2' to identifiers in the generic machine.
elements.sent-2411-226: Beyond instantiations, the project file contains SYNCH lines.
elements.sent-2411-227: These specify collections of mode events across multiple machines that must be scheduled simultaneously (i.e.~only when all their guards are simultaneously true).
elements.sent-2411-228: Thus `SYNCH $\bf ActivateDrone1$' enforces the simultaneous execution of event { \fbox{ \parbox{0.48\linewidth { *{2ex PROJECT~~$\bf IncidentResponse\_Prj$\rule{0ex {2.5ex *{2ex GLOBINVS~~$\bf IncidentResponse\_GI$ *{2ex CONTEXT~~$\bf IncidentResponse\_CTX$ *{2ex CONTEXT~~$\bf Controller\_CTX$ *{2ex CONTEXT~~$\bf Drone1\_CTX$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Drone\_CTX}$~~WITH *{6ex}$Vdr \rightarrow Vdr1$ *{4ex END *{2ex CONTEXT~~$\bf Drone2\_CTX$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Drone\_CTX}$~~WITH *{6ex}$Vdr \rightarrow Vdr2$ *{4ex END *{2ex CONTEXT~~$\bf Responder\_CTX$ *{2ex INTERFACE~~$\bf IncidentResponse\_IF$ *{2ex INTERFACE~~$\bf ControllerDrones\_IF$ *{2ex INTERFACE~~$\bf ControllerResponder\_IF$ *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Controller\_Mch$ *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf EnvironmentScenario\_Mch$ *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Drone1\_Mch$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Drone\_Mch}$~~WITH *{6ex}$\bullet\bullet\bullet$ *{4ex END *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Drone2\_Mch$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Drone\_Mch}$~~WITH *{6ex}$\bullet\bullet\bullet$ *{4ex END *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Responder1\_Mch$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Responder\_Mch}$~~WITH *{6ex}$\bullet\bullet\bullet$ *{4ex END *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Responder2\_Mch$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Responder\_Mch}$~~WITH *{6ex}$\bullet\bullet\bullet$ *{4ex END *{2ex}\ldots~~~~\ldots \raisebox{-1ex {\rule{0pt {2.5ex \fbox{ \parbox{0.48\linewidth { *{2ex}\ldots~~~~\ldots\rule{0ex {2.5ex *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Responder3\_Mch$~~IS *{4ex}$\mathit{Responder\_Mch}$~~WITH *{6ex}$\bullet\bullet\bullet$ *{4ex END *{2ex SYNCH $\bf ActivateDrone1$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Controller\_Mch.LaunchDrone1}$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Drone1\_Mch.Activate1}$ *{4ex END *{2ex SYNCH $\bf UpdateDrone1$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Controller\_Mch.UpdateDrone1}$ SYNCH~~~$\bullet\bullet\bullet~~~\bf Drone2$ *{2ex SYNCH $\bf ActivateResponder1$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Controller\_Mch.LaunchResponder1}$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Responder1\_Mch.Activate1}$ *{4ex END *{2ex SYNCH $\bf UpdateResponder1$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Controller\_Mch.UpdateResponder1}$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Responder1\_Mch.Update1}$ *{4ex END *{2ex SYNCH $\bf DeActivateResponder1$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Controller\_Mch.RecallResponder1}$ *{6ex}$\mathit{Responder1\_Mch.DeActivate1)}$ *{4ex END *{2ex SYNCH~~~$\bullet\bullet\bullet~~~\bf Responder2$ *{2ex SYNCH~~~$\bullet\bullet\bullet~~~\bf Responder3$ *{2ex END \raisebox{-1ex {\rule{0pt {2.5ex $\bf LaunchDrone1$ in the $\bf Controller\_Mch$ machine and of $\bf Activate1$ in the $\bf Drone1\_Mch$ machine.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: There are similar synchronisations for $\bf Upate$ing and for $\bf DeActivate$ing the $\bf Drone1\_Mch$ machine.
elements.sent-2411-230: The analogous synchronisations for the $\bf Drone2\_Mch$ machine are suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-231: A similar pattern applies to the responders.
elements.sent-2411-232: The details for the $\bf Responder1\_Mch$ machine are given in full, while those for machines $\bf Responder2\_Mch$ and $\bf Responder3\_Mch$ are suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-233: Essentially, it is a finite state machine, perfectly expressible using Event-B alone if need be, except that the availability of real time for scheduling purposes simplifies the state space that would otherwise be needed.
elements.sent-2411-234: The main job of the controller, once activated (event $\mathit{ActivateController}$) is: to send forth the drones and responders (events $\mathit{LaunchDrone1}$, $\mathit{LaunchDrone2}$, $\mathit{LaunchResp1}$, $\mathit{LaunchResp2}$, $\mathit{LaunchResp3}$), to poll the environment machine to discover changes to the collection of hazards that have been unearthed (events $\mathit{StartMonitoring}$, $\mathit{MonitorHazardsNull}$, $\mathit{MonitorHazardsUpdate}$) and to advise the drones and responders of the same (events $\mathit{UpdateDrone1}$, $\mathit{UpdateDrone2}$, $\mathit{UpdateResp1}$, $\mathit{UpdateResp2}$, $\mathit{UpdateResp3}$), and finally to recall the drones and responders when the work has been completed (events $\mathit{RecallDrone1}$, $\mathit{RecallDrone2}$, $\mathit{RecallResp1}$, $\mathit{RecallResp2}$, $\mathit{RecallResp3}$).
elements.sent-2411-235: Once this is done, the controller is deactivated (event $\mathit{DeActivateController}$).
elements.sent-2411-236: A default $\mathit{PliTrue}$ pliant event covers the gaps between state change.
elements.sent-2411-237: In the above, there are clearly groups of similar events that need to take place periodically (launching, monitoring, updating, recalling) for agents that are largely independent of each other most of the time.
elements.sent-2411-238: Achieving this using a pure finite state machine would entail either a state explosion to accommodate all possible interleavings of individual agent events, or an excessive use of sequentialisation to avoid it.
elements.sent-2411-239: The presence of time as an intrinsic feature in Hybrid Event-B (assumed synchronised across all machines) permits a more economical approach.
elements.sent-2411-240: A `large' time window $\Delta$ is defined along with a `small' window $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-241: Launching is defined to take place within the first $\delta$ of elapsed time, via synchronised events, as described above.
elements.sent-2411-242: Since the main synchronisation mechanism is the time interval of duration $\delta$, arbitrary orderings of the launching events within that interval are permitted.
elements.sent-2411-243: The same technique is used for all the other synchronised activities.
elements.sent-2411-244: Monitoring and update take place repeatedly after the elapse of each period of duration $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-245: At $t = n\,\Delta$, the EnvironmentScenario machine potentially updates the $hazards$.
elements.sent-2411-246: Within the next $\delta/2$ period, the controller checks whether $hazards$ has changed since the last check, and if so, schedules updates to the drones and responders to update their hazard data and to replan if necessary.
elements.sent-2411-247: To keep the model simple, this is accomplished by setting the $drones2comd$ and $resps2comd$ variables to the IDs of all the drones and responders.
elements.sent-2411-248: This triggers, in the next $\delta/2$ period, each of them to update it's local copy of the $hazards$ variable via a synchronised event such as e.g.,~the combination of $\mathit{UpdateDrone1}$ in the controller together with $\mathit{Update}$ in first drone.
elements.sent-2411-249: Once that has happened, the drone or responder can recalculate its trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-250: This is achieved by the same synchonisation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-251: In other words, after $\mathit{DURATION}$ has elapsed, similar windows of length $\delta$ are established and the drones and responders adopt first the $\mathit{RETURN}$ mode, and after returning home, the $\mathit{OFF}$ mode.
elements.sent-2411-252: The generic drone machine $\bf Drone\_Mch$ appears in the two panels on the previous page.
elements.sent-2411-253: Once a drone is activated through the synchronised $\mathit{Activate}$ event, it ceases the default $\mathit{PliTrue}$ behaviour and instead pursues the $\mathit{Navigate}$ behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-254: The preceding mode event occurrence, whether an $\mathit{Activate}$ or an $\mathit{Update}$ event, caused it to remember its 3D position at that time in variables $thex$, $they$, $thez$, from { \fbox{ \parbox{0.48\linewidth { *{2ex MACHINE~~$\bf Responder\_Mch$\rule{0ex {2.5ex *{2ex SEES~~$\bf Responder\_CTX$ *{2ex CONNECTS~~$\bf ControllerResponder\_IF$ *{2ex VARIABLES *{4ex}$mode$ *{4ex}$thex~,~they$ *{4ex}$trajectory$ *{2ex PLIANT *{4ex}$respx~,~respy$ *{2ex INVARIANTS *{4ex}$mode : \mathit{RESPSTATE}$ *{4ex}$thex~,~they : \mathbb{R ~,~\mathbb{R}$ *{4ex}$respx~,~respy : \mathbb{R ~,~\mathbb{R}$ *{4ex}$trajectory : \mathrm{seq (\mathbb{R \times \mathbb{R )$ *{2ex EVENTS *{4ex}$\mathit{INITIALISATION}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$mode$ ~:=~ $\mathit{OFF}$ *{8ex}$thex~,~they$ ~:=~ $0~,~0$ *{8ex}$respx~,~respy$ ~:=~ $0~,~0$ *{8ex}$trajectory$ ~:=~ $\langle\,\rangle$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{PliTrue}$ *{6ex STATUS~~pliant *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = \mathit{OFF}$ *{6ex COMPLY *{8ex}$\mathit{INVARIANTS}$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{Activate}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = \mathit{OFF}$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$mode$ ~:=~ $\mathit{SEEK}$ *{8ex}$thex~,~they$ ~:=~ $respx~,~respy$ *{8ex}$trajectory$ ~:=~ $calcTraj(\ldots)$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{Update}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode \in \{\mathit{SEEK ~,~\mathit{RETURN}\}$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$thex~,~they$ ~:=~ $respx~,~respy$ *{8ex}$trajectory$ ~:=~ $calcTraj(\ldots)$ *{6ex END *{2ex}\ldots~~~~\ldots \raisebox{-1ex {\rule{0pt {2.5ex \fbox{ \parbox{0.48\linewidth { *{2ex}\ldots~~~~\ldots\rule{0ex {2.5ex *{4ex}$\mathit{Navigate}$ *{6ex STATUS~~pliant *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode \in \{\mathit{SEEK ~,~\mathit{RETURN}\}~\land$ *{8ex}$trajectory \neq \langle\,\rangle$ *{6ex SOLVE *{8ex}$\mathscr{D respx$ ~:=~ $V\!dr \times (\mathrm{first (trajectory)[1] - thex)$ *{8ex}$\mathscr{D respy$ ~:=~ $V\!dr \times (\mathrm{first (trajectory)[2] - they)$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{Waypoint}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode \in \{\mathit{SEEK ~,~\mathit{RETURN}\}~\land$ *{8ex}$respx = \mathrm{first (trajectory)[1]~\land$ *{8ex}$respy = \mathrm{first (trajectory)[2]~\land$ *{8ex}$trajectory \neq \langle\,\rangle$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$thex~,~they$ ~:=~ $respx~,~respy$ *{8ex}$trajectory$ ~:=~ $\mathrm{rest (trajectory)$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{Arrived}$ *{6ex STATUS~~asynch *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = SEEK \land trajectory = \langle\,\rangle$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$mode$ ~:=~ $ARRIVED$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{DoSomethingForAWhile}$ *{6ex STATUS~~pliant *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = ARRIVED$ *{6ex COMPLY *{8ex}$\mathit{INVARIANTS}$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{DeActivate}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = \mathit{ARRIVED}$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$mode$ ~:=~ $\mathit{RETURN}$ *{8ex}$thex~,~they$ ~:=~ $respx~,~respy$ *{8ex}$trajectory$ ~:=~ $calcTraj(\ldots)$ *{6ex END *{4ex}$\mathit{SwitchOff}$ *{6ex STATUS~~ordinary *{6ex WHEN *{8ex}$mode = \mathit{RETURN ~\land$ *{8ex}$respx = respy = respz = 0$ *{6ex BEGIN *{8ex}$mode$ ~:=~ $\mathit{OFF}$ *{6ex END *{2ex END \raisebox{-1ex {\rule{0pt {2.5ex which it can calculate a trajectory towards its goal.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: The tactic taken is to navigate towards the centroid of the positions of the controller and responders.
elements.sent-2411-256: The two drones need to ensure that they avoid damage in a hazardous area, so if they need to fly close to one, they need to take heed of its geometry, including height.
elements.sent-2411-257: Also, to avoid simultaneous destruction of both the drones though mishap, they need to ensure that they stay far enough apart from each other.
elements.sent-2411-258: This is all done by the $\mathit{calcCentAvoidTraj (\ldots)$ function that they use, except that, in this paper, we abstract away from the detailed calculations that would be needed to achieve this.}
elements.sent-2411-259: Evidently, the drones need to share their positions to do all this, so they share a single interface which declares both, which they can use to exchange positions by communicating with each other.
elements.sent-2411-260: For reasons of brevity, in this paper we do not cover this aspect in detail either.
elements.sent-2411-261: All of this missing detail could be handled via a suitable Hybrid Event-B refinement.
elements.sent-2411-262: For simplicity again, a trajectory calculated by the drone consists of a sequence of line segments between points in three dimensions, which the drones follow, in order.
elements.sent-2411-263: The traversal of each segment is accomplished within the $\mathit{Navigate}$ event.
elements.sent-2411-264: This simply increments each of the spatial coordinates, using the drone velocity (which is statically defined in its context) in proportion to the coordinate difference to be traversed.
elements.sent-2411-265: Once a segment is completed, the first segment is deleted from the trajectory in the $\mathit{Waypoint}$ event, and the drone follows the next segment, until there is no trajectory left, at which point the drone stays where it is, maintaining distance from its partner.
elements.sent-2411-266: The last big machine of the development is the generic responder machine $\bf Responder\_Mch$ which appears on the previous page.
elements.sent-2411-267: This is similar to the drone machine in most respects, notably the technique used for synchronisation with the controller, but with a few essential differences.
elements.sent-2411-268: For example, the responders stay on the ground, so do not need a $z$ coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-269: They are also assumed to not need to communicate with each other regarding position, so in our model, each responder can have its own interface.
elements.sent-2411-270: They do not need to fly above hazards, so their trajectory calculations will be different, expressed in the function $\mathit{calcTraj (\ldots)$.}
elements.sent-2411-271: And once arrived, they do some work until they, along with the drones, are recalled by the controller, which is achieved using the customary mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-272: The main purpose of the Hybrid Event-B approach is the verification of models expressed within the formalism, in order to derive increased confidence about their dependability.
elements.sent-2411-273: As for its predecessors in the classical B-Method and Event-B, the emphasis is on safety properties, expressed within the invariants declared in the model's syntactic constructs.
elements.sent-2411-274: In the case of Hybrid Event-B, these can be found inside machines and interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-275: At the time of writing, there is no tool support for Hybrid Event-B, so a comprehensive mechanically supported verification exercise cannot be reported on.
elements.sent-2411-276: Nevertheless, the POs that define correctness in Hybrid Event-B have been investigated in detail in the papers cited earlier, so an outline of what would be involved can be given.
elements.sent-2411-277: Two things deserve to be highlighted.
elements.sent-2411-278: The first is that, as indicated earlier, the design of multi-machine Hybrid Event-B is such that the syntactic scope of all the POs is precisely defined by the rules of construction that models have to conform to.
elements.sent-2411-279: The second is that, the present model has deliberately been designed to be so simple, that the overwhelming majority of POs become rather trivial.
elements.sent-2411-280: One set of POs concerns feasibility: i.e., is there an after-state for every enabled mode event? The simple assignments to constants in their bodies says yes; likewise for pliant events, which have either trivial COMPLY {\it INVARIANTS bodies, or merely specify the following of a linear trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Another major set of POs concerns the preservation of the invariants.
elements.sent-2411-282: Since, by design, these are almost exclusively trivial typing declarations, the answer will again be yes.
elements.sent-2411-283: Such POs can be dealt with by inspection.
elements.sent-2411-284: Slightly more complicated are POs that deal with correct handover: mode/pliant and pliant/mode.
elements.sent-2411-285: This involves calculating disjunctions of guards, to ensure that when an event completes, an event of the right kind can succeede it.
elements.sent-2411-286: Again, extreme simplicity makes this a task that can be done by hand.
elements.sent-2411-287: So we can be confident our model is as it should be, even if mechanical corroboration would be even better.
elements.sent-2411-288: The preceding remarks prepare the ground for discussing the last construct of the project, the global invariants $\bf IncidentResponse\_GI$.
elements.sent-2411-289: The global invariants offer the possibility of explicitly stating invariants of the system that are properties of the system as a whole, and not merely of some part of it.
elements.sent-2411-290: Clearly such a capability is important when safety properties depend on the safe cooperation of all parts of the system.
elements.sent-2411-291: Global invariants are intended to be derivable from the remaining invariants of a correct model, but this is not a hard constraint.
elements.sent-2411-292: The intention is that global invariants would be checked in the latter stages of a development, once the correctness of the constituent parts of the project is established.
elements.sent-2411-293: It states that outside the periods when the individual machines' perceptions of the hazard configurations are being updated (which are the time intervals of length $\delta$ following those integral multiples of $\Delta$ defined in the $\mathit{INITSCHED}$ constant of the IncidentResponse context), they all agree on the hazards.
elements.sent-2411-294: The conclusion of the invariant is a conjunction of statements each of which is a property of a part of the system.
elements.sent-2411-295: So it cannot be proved before all the pieces have been successfully constructed.
elements.sent-2411-296: A technical detail is that, for simplicity, the individual conjuncts (appropriately guarded) were not included as invariants of the relevant machines above.
elements.sent-2411-297: This itself does not make the global invariant unprovable of course.
elements.sent-2411-298: Global invariants, in particular, underline the connection between the Hybrid Event-B approach to system correctness and the reference to safety properties in this paper's title.
elements.sent-2411-299: In the early part of the paper, we surveyed Hybrid Event-B and how it evolved from Event-B.
elements.sent-2411-300: The inclusion of real time and smooth state change entails considerable additional technical complexity in the semantics, and we discussed the details of this to the extent that space permitted.
elements.sent-2411-301: Multi-machine working brings in a whole raft of additional technical details to worry about, principally concerned with synchronisations and with the interplay of structure and verification needs.
elements.sent-2411-302: We surveyed this as far as possible.
elements.sent-2411-303: The multi-machine Hybrid Event-B approach is particularly suited to the formalisation and verification of autonomous systems, as these systems are seldom isolated, must fend for themselves for long periods of time while nevertheless communicating, but only intermittently, and are often cyber-physical.
elements.sent-2411-304: So modelling them naturally partitions into a constellation of cooperating but largely self-contained Hybrid Event-B machines, supported by suitable syntactic constructs.
elements.sent-2411-305: The point of all this was to lay the groundwork for a small multi-machine example, to support the case just made.
elements.sent-2411-306: A simple system of intermittently communicating drones, responders and a controller was modelled.
elements.sent-2411-307: It is reasonable to say that this illustrated convincingly the capabilities of multi-machine Hybrid Event-B to fluently capture the kinds of behaviour pattern needed in such systems.. \bibliographystyle{eptcs .}
elements.section-2411-008: Appendix: Contexts, Interfaces and Instantiated Machine Outlines
